live_messages.content:
You are helping build onto-canon6, a review-first canonical assertion
system.

Downstream, every candidate assertion you produce will be:

- validated against an ontology profile
- reviewed by a human or agent before acceptance
- inspected through exact evidence spans in the original source text

The system is more useful when it receives a very small set of strong
candidates than when it receives a longer noisy list.

Return only candidate assertions that are directly supported by the text.
Return exactly one structured response object with one `candidates`
array. Put every supported candidate inside that single array. Never
split the answer across multiple tool calls or multiple top-level
responses.

Return at most 10 candidates.
Use at most 1 evidence spans per
candidate.

Prefer the smallest sufficient candidate set. Keep only the strongest,
most review-worthy typed facts. A shorter correct response is better than
a longer speculative one.

Prefer explicit actor-action-object facts over analytical summaries,
evaluative narration, or conclusion prose. A paragraph that summarizes
effectiveness, limitations, need for reform, or overall trajectory is
usually restating analysis about earlier facts, not adding a new typed
assertion.

Empty candidates are allowed. If you cannot return a candidate with a
non-empty `roles` object grounded directly in the source text, return an
empty `candidates` array instead of a partial candidate.

For each candidate:

- give the ontology-shaped assertion as `predicate` plus `roles`
- keep `predicate` to the predicate ID only; put filler data only in the
  sibling `roles` object
- include exact `evidence_spans.text` copied character-for-character
  from the source
- optionally include a short `claim_text` gloss for review

`roles` must map each role name to an array of fillers. Even a single
filler must appear inside a one-element array, not as a bare object.

Every filler object must include `kind` set to `entity`, `value`, or
`unknown`.

Every `kind: value` filler must include `value_kind` and `raw` or
`normalized`.

Use source punctuation directly inside string values. Do not invent
backslash escapes for apostrophes or quotes beyond normal JSON string
encoding.

Use exact source surface forms for entity names and values. If the text
names something only by an acronym, alternate designation, or quoted
label, use that surface form as the filler name or value. Do not leave a
role empty while waiting for a more canonical name.

Build candidates role-first. Identify concrete role fillers from the
source text before choosing the predicate. If you cannot fill at least
one role concretely, omit the entire candidate.

Omit a candidate if:

- the entity cannot be named and typed from the source
- any role would be missing or empty
- the fact is merely implied rather than stated clearly
- the candidate would duplicate a stronger candidate already present
- the candidate is minor detail rather than one of the main typed facts
- the sentence is only analytical narration or evaluative summary about
  an organization, campaign, or period without explicitly naming the
  speaker or limited subject in the same span

Never emit `roles: {}`. If a candidate cannot carry at least one filled
role from the source, omit it entirely.

Use the active ontology catalog when it fits the text. If the text
clearly supports a predicate outside the current catalog, propose the
strongest descriptive `oc:*` predicate you can.

Use the following error checks before you return a candidate:

- abbreviation expansions, acronym definitions, alternate names, and
  parenthetical name expansions usually identify the same entity with
  multiple names, not a relationship or a `replace_designation` event
- descriptive appositives and role labels usually describe the same
  named entity, not a separate parent organization; do not turn those
  descriptive phrases into `belongs_to_organization` fillers unless the
  text explicitly names a distinct organization that the entity belongs
  to
- `hold_command_role` is for a named person holding a role over an
  organization; subordinate-unit text, parent/child organization text,
  and organizational membership text usually belong under
  `belongs_to_organization`
- a bare mention that a unit, task force, or organization was
  established is usually background context, not a reviewable
  `create_organizational_unit` event; omit it unless the source directly
  states a concrete creation fact with the needed named roles
- MOS codes, classifications, labels, and taxonomy terms are usually
  values or aliases, not standalone entities that belong to an
  organization
- a named institution, review, or report can be the speaker for
  `express_concern` only when the same evidence span explicitly names
  that institution, review, or report and attributes the concern,
  warning, finding, criticism, or dissatisfaction to it
- analytical narration, report voice, and author commentary are not
  named speakers; do not use the organization under discussion as the
  `express_concern` speaker unless the sentence explicitly attributes the
  concern to that organization or another named speaker
- do not invent generic or likely institutions such as `Congressional
  oversight`, `oversight bodies`, `critics`, or `commanders` unless the
  cited evidence span itself names them
- oversight, scrutiny, periodic review, hearings, investigation, or
  doctrine adjustment are not automatically `express_concern`; treat
  them as governance process context unless the same evidence span
  explicitly states a concern, warning, criticism, or dissatisfaction
- conclusion sections, opinion sections, and summary prose usually
  restate overall assessment; do not extract `express_concern` from them
  unless a named institution, review, or person is explicitly presented
  as the speaker inside the same evidence span
- sentences like `effectiveness was limited`, `limitations observed`,
  `questions were raised`, or `need for reform` are usually analytical
  assessment, not a typed concern event; unless the same evidence span
  explicitly says who raised the concern, omit it
- if the text only implies sentiment, concern, or dissatisfaction without
  a named speaker, return no opinion candidate rather than inventing a
  person or vague source
- `limit_capability` requires an explicitly named limited subject from
  the source text; if the sentence only describes friction, challenges,
  impediments, or abstract effectiveness in general analytical prose
  without a clean named subject and capability in the same span, omit
  the candidate instead of anchoring it loosely
- do not inherit the document's main organization, campaign, or topic as
  the `express_concern` speaker or `limit_capability` subject from
  section context alone; the speaker or limited subject must be explicit
  in the same evidence span you cite

Return only the structured candidate payload. Do not add explanatory
prose before or after it.

Extract candidate assertions from this source text.

Active profile:
- profile_id: psyop_seed
- profile_version: 0.1.0

Active predicate catalog:
- oc:belongs_to_organization (roles: member [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:military_unit]; organization [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:military_organization])
- oc:create_organizational_unit (roles: created_unit [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:military_unit]; parent_organization [optional, count=0..1, entity_type=oc:military_organization])
- oc:criticize_change (roles: complaint [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string]; critic [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:community]; target_change [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string])
- oc:describe_dissatisfaction (roles: description [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string]; speaker [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:community]; subject [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:community])
- oc:express_concern (roles: speaker [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:community]; subject [optional, count=0..1, entity_type=oc:psychological_operation]; topic [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string])
- oc:hold_command_role (roles: commander [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:person]; organization [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:military_organization]; role_title [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string])
- oc:limit_capability (roles: capability [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string]; subject [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:psychological_operation]; topic [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string])
- oc:operation_occurs_in_location (roles: location [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string]; operation [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:military_operation])
- oc:replace_designation (roles: actor [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:person]; new_designation [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string]; old_designation [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string]; subject [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:psychological_operation]; time [optional, count=0..1, value_kind=time])
- oc:seek_exit_from_field (roles: label [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string]; subject [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:community])
- oc:send_report (roles: message [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:report]; recipient [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:government_organization]; sender [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:military_unit])
- oc:use_organizational_form (roles: location [optional, count=0..1, value_kind=string]; operation [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:military_operation]; organizational_form [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:military_unit])
- oc:uses_designation_label (roles: label [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string]; subject [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:psychological_operation])
- oc:view_as_classification (roles: actor [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:military_organization]; label [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string]; subject [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:information_content])

Active entity-type catalog:
- oc:community
- oc:government_organization
- oc:information_content
- oc:military_operation
- oc:military_organization
- oc:military_unit
- oc:organization
- oc:person
- oc:psychological_operation
- oc:report
- oc:text_document

Source text:
#### Table 2: USSOCOM Commanders (2001–2015)

| Years        | Commander                       | Service Branch |
|--------------|--------------------------------|---------------|
| 2000–2003    | Gen. Charles R. Holland        | USAF          |
| 2003–2007    | Gen. Bryan D. Brown            | USA           |
| 2007–2011    | Adm. Eric T. Olson             | USN           |
| 2011–2014    | Adm. William H. McRaven        | USN           |
| 2014–2016    | Gen. Joseph L. Votel           | USA           |

([Wikipedia, USSOCOM](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States_Special_Operations_Command))

### PSYOP Group Commanders

The 4th PSYOP Group, as the principal Army PSYOP unit, had its own commanders, but their names are not widely available in open literature due to the sensitive nature of their work. However, the command philosophy and operational priorities were shaped by the broader USSOCOM leadership and the evolving requirements of the War on Terror.

## Key Psychological Operations Programs (2001–2015)

### 1. Operation Enduring Freedom (Afghanistan)

PSYOP played a critical role in Afghanistan, supporting both conventional and special operations forces. Key activities included leaflet drops, radio broadcasts, and face-to-face engagements designed to undermine Taliban influence, encourage defections, and support coalition legitimacy. The use of the EC-130J Commando Solo aircraft for broadcasting messages in local languages was a hallmark of this period ([Wikipedia, US PSYOP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psychological_operations_(United_States))).

### 2. Operation Iraqi Freedom (Iraq)

In Iraq, PSYOP was integral to both the initial invasion and subsequent counterinsurgency operations. Programs included the dissemination of surrender appeals, the targeting of insurgent networks with disinformation, and the support of civil-military operations to win “hearts and minds.” The Joint PSYOP Task Force model was employed to coordinate efforts across multiple coalition partners ([Wikipedia, US PSYOP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psychological_operations_(United_States))).

### 3. Joint PSYOP Task Force and Integration

A significant innovation during this period was the establishment of Joint PSYOP Task Forces (JPOTF), which allowed for the integration of PSYOP into all phases of campaign planning and execution. This approach was notably successful during Operation Restore Hope in Somalia and was adapted for use in Afghanistan and Iraq ([Wikipedia, US PSYOP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psychological_operations_(United_States))).

### 4. Strategic Communication and Information Operations

From 2005 onward, there was a growing emphasis on integrating PSYOP with broader information operations (IO) and strategic communication efforts. This reflected a recognition that military messaging needed to be synchronized with diplomatic, economic, and informational lines of effort. The Commander’s Handbook for Strategic Communication (2010) formalized many of these practices ([Wikipedia, US PSYOP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psychological_operations_(United_States))).

### 5. Technological Innovation

The period saw the increasing use of digital media, social networks, and mobile technology in PSYOP campaigns. The rise of the internet and satellite communications allowed for more targeted and rapid dissemination of messages, both to adversary populations and to global audiences.

### 6. Lessons Learned and Institutional Reform

A 2005 review by the National Defense University highlighted both successes and persistent challenges in PSYOP, including the need for better integration with other elements of national power and improved measurement of effectiveness ([Lamb & Genalis, 2005](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psychological_operations_(United_States))).

## Critical Analysis

#### Table 2: USSOCOM Commanders (2001–2015)

| Years        | Commander                       | Service Branch |
|--------------|--------------------------------|---------------|
| 2000–2003    | Gen. Charles R. Holland        | USAF          |
| 2003–2007    | Gen. Bryan D. Brown            | USA           |
| 2007–2011    | Adm. Eric T. Olson             | USN           |
| 2011–2014    | Adm. William H. McRaven        | USN           |
| 2014–2016    | Gen. Joseph L. Votel           | USA           |

([Wikipedia, USSOCOM](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States_Special_Operations_Command))

### PSYOP Group Commanders

The 4th PSYOP Group, as the principal Army PSYOP unit, had its own commanders, but their names are not widely available in open literature due to the sensitive nature of their work. However, the command philosophy and operational priorities were shaped by the broader USSOCOM leadership and the evolving requirements of the War on Terror.

## Key Psychological Operations Programs (2001–2015)

PSYOP played a critical role in Afghanistan, supporting both conventional and special operations forces. ### 2. Operation Iraqi Freedom (Iraq)

### 3. Joint PSYOP Task Force and Integration

A significant innovation during this period was the establishment of Joint PSYOP Task Forces (JPOTF), which allowed for the integration of PSYOP into all phases of campaign planning and execution. This approach was notably successful during Operation Restore Hope in Somalia and was adapted for use in Afghanistan and Iraq ([Wikipedia, US PSYOP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psychological_operations_(United_States))).

### 4. Strategic Communication and Information Operations

From 2005 onward, there was a growing emphasis on integrating PSYOP with broader information operations (IO) and strategic communication efforts. The Commander’s Handbook for Strategic Communication (2010) formalized many of these practices ([Wikipedia, US PSYOP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psychological_operations_(United_States))).

The Commander’s Handbook for Strategic Communication (2010) formalized many of these practices ([Wikipedia, US PSYOP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psychological_operations_(United_States))).

### 5. Technological Innovation

The period saw the increasing use of digital media, social networks, and mobile technology in PSYOP campaigns. The rise of the internet and satellite communications allowed for more targeted and rapid dissemination of messages, both to adversary populations and to global audiences.

### 6. Lessons Learned and Institutional Reform

A 2005 review by the National Defense University highlighted both successes and persistent challenges in PSYOP, including the need for better integration with other elements of national power and improved measurement of effectiveness ([Lamb & Genalis, 2005](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psychological_operations_(United_States))).

## Critical Analysis

prompt_eval_template_messages.content:
You are helping build onto-canon6, a review-first canonical assertion
system.

Downstream, every candidate assertion you produce will be:

- validated against an ontology profile
- reviewed by a human or agent before acceptance
- inspected through exact evidence spans in the original source text

The system is more useful when it receives a very small set of strong
candidates than when it receives a longer noisy list.

Return only candidate assertions that are directly supported by the text.
Return exactly one structured response object with one `candidates`
array. Put every supported candidate inside that single array. Never
split the answer across multiple tool calls or multiple top-level
responses.

Return at most 10 candidates.
Use at most 1 evidence spans per
candidate.

Prefer the smallest sufficient candidate set. Keep only the strongest,
most review-worthy typed facts. A shorter correct response is better than
a longer speculative one.

Prefer explicit actor-action-object facts over analytical summaries,
evaluative narration, or conclusion prose. A paragraph that summarizes
effectiveness, limitations, need for reform, or overall trajectory is
usually restating analysis about earlier facts, not adding a new typed
assertion.

Empty candidates are allowed. If you cannot return a candidate with a
non-empty `roles` object grounded directly in the source text, return an
empty `candidates` array instead of a partial candidate.

For each candidate:

- give the ontology-shaped assertion as `predicate` plus `roles`
- keep `predicate` to the predicate ID only; put filler data only in the
  sibling `roles` object
- include exact `evidence_spans.text` copied character-for-character
  from the source
- optionally include a short `claim_text` gloss for review

`roles` must map each role name to an array of fillers. Even a single
filler must appear inside a one-element array, not as a bare object.

Every filler object must include `kind` set to `entity`, `value`, or
`unknown`.

Every `kind: value` filler must include `value_kind` and `raw` or
`normalized`.

Use source punctuation directly inside string values. Do not invent
backslash escapes for apostrophes or quotes beyond normal JSON string
encoding.

Use exact source surface forms for entity names and values. If the text
names something only by an acronym, alternate designation, or quoted
label, use that surface form as the filler name or value. Do not leave a
role empty while waiting for a more canonical name.

Build candidates role-first. Identify concrete role fillers from the
source text before choosing the predicate. If you cannot fill at least
one role concretely, omit the entire candidate.

Omit a candidate if:

- the entity cannot be named and typed from the source
- any role would be missing or empty
- the fact is merely implied rather than stated clearly
- the candidate would duplicate a stronger candidate already present
- the candidate is minor detail rather than one of the main typed facts
- the sentence is only analytical narration or evaluative summary about
  an organization, campaign, or period without explicitly naming the
  speaker or limited subject in the same span

Never emit `roles: {}`. If a candidate cannot carry at least one filled
role from the source, omit it entirely.

Use the active ontology catalog when it fits the text. If the text
clearly supports a predicate outside the current catalog, propose the
strongest descriptive `oc:*` predicate you can.

Use the following error checks before you return a candidate:

- abbreviation expansions, acronym definitions, alternate names, and
  parenthetical name expansions usually identify the same entity with
  multiple names, not a relationship or a `replace_designation` event
- descriptive appositives and role labels usually describe the same
  named entity, not a separate parent organization; do not turn those
  descriptive phrases into `belongs_to_organization` fillers unless the
  text explicitly names a distinct organization that the entity belongs
  to
- `hold_command_role` is for a named person holding a role over an
  organization; subordinate-unit text, parent/child organization text,
  and organizational membership text usually belong under
  `belongs_to_organization`
- a bare mention that a unit, task force, or organization was
  established is usually background context, not a reviewable
  `create_organizational_unit` event; omit it unless the source directly
  states a concrete creation fact with the needed named roles
- MOS codes, classifications, labels, and taxonomy terms are usually
  values or aliases, not standalone entities that belong to an
  organization
- a named institution, review, or report can be the speaker for
  `express_concern` only when the same evidence span explicitly names
  that institution, review, or report and attributes the concern,
  warning, finding, criticism, or dissatisfaction to it
- analytical narration, report voice, and author commentary are not
  named speakers; do not use the organization under discussion as the
  `express_concern` speaker unless the sentence explicitly attributes the
  concern to that organization or another named speaker
- do not invent generic or likely institutions such as `Congressional
  oversight`, `oversight bodies`, `critics`, or `commanders` unless the
  cited evidence span itself names them
- oversight, scrutiny, periodic review, hearings, investigation, or
  doctrine adjustment are not automatically `express_concern`; treat
  them as governance process context unless the same evidence span
  explicitly states a concern, warning, criticism, or dissatisfaction
- conclusion sections, opinion sections, and summary prose usually
  restate overall assessment; do not extract `express_concern` from them
  unless a named institution, review, or person is explicitly presented
  as the speaker inside the same evidence span
- sentences like `effectiveness was limited`, `limitations observed`,
  `questions were raised`, or `need for reform` are usually analytical
  assessment, not a typed concern event; unless the same evidence span
  explicitly says who raised the concern, omit it
- if the text only implies sentiment, concern, or dissatisfaction without
  a named speaker, return no opinion candidate rather than inventing a
  person or vague source
- `limit_capability` requires an explicitly named limited subject from
  the source text; if the sentence only describes friction, challenges,
  impediments, or abstract effectiveness in general analytical prose
  without a clean named subject and capability in the same span, omit
  the candidate instead of anchoring it loosely
- do not inherit the document's main organization, campaign, or topic as
  the `express_concern` speaker or `limit_capability` subject from
  section context alone; the speaker or limited subject must be explicit
  in the same evidence span you cite

Return only the structured candidate payload. Do not add explanatory
prose before or after it.

Extract candidate assertions from this source material.

Active profile:
- profile_id: psyop_seed
- profile_version: 0.1.0

Active predicate catalog:
- oc:belongs_to_organization (roles: member [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:military_unit]; organization [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:military_organization])
- oc:create_organizational_unit (roles: created_unit [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:military_unit]; parent_organization [optional, count=0..1, entity_type=oc:military_organization])
- oc:criticize_change (roles: complaint [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string]; critic [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:community]; target_change [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string])
- oc:describe_dissatisfaction (roles: description [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string]; speaker [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:community]; subject [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:community])
- oc:express_concern (roles: speaker [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:community]; subject [optional, count=0..1, entity_type=oc:psychological_operation]; topic [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string])
- oc:hold_command_role (roles: commander [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:person]; organization [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:military_organization]; role_title [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string])
- oc:limit_capability (roles: capability [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string]; subject [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:psychological_operation]; topic [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string])
- oc:operation_occurs_in_location (roles: location [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string]; operation [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:military_operation])
- oc:replace_designation (roles: actor [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:person]; new_designation [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string]; old_designation [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string]; subject [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:psychological_operation]; time [optional, count=0..1, value_kind=time])
- oc:seek_exit_from_field (roles: label [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string]; subject [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:community])
- oc:send_report (roles: message [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:report]; recipient [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:government_organization]; sender [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:military_unit])
- oc:use_organizational_form (roles: location [optional, count=0..1, value_kind=string]; operation [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:military_operation]; organizational_form [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:military_unit])
- oc:uses_designation_label (roles: label [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string]; subject [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:psychological_operation])
- oc:view_as_classification (roles: actor [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:military_organization]; label [required, count=1..1, value_kind=string]; subject [required, count=1..1, entity_type=oc:information_content])

Active entity-type catalog:

- oc:information_content

- oc:military_organization
- oc:military_unit
- oc:organization
